coredata.dc:title: Lithium-ion point-of-care ultrasound battery joint state of charge estimation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ndzana, Nicolas Daniel Mbele
Nelem, Aristide Tolok
Abanda, Yannick Antoine
Pesdjock, Mathieu Jean Pierre
Ngouagna, Murele Vanina Toukam
Zeh, Odile Fernande
Ele, Pierre
coredata.dc:description: With recent advances in ultrasound technology, including accessibility and portability, point-of-care ultrasound systems (POCUS) are becoming ubiquitous in urban and rural health services. The efficient use of POCUS battery power is necessary due to the limited access to electricity in rural locations. Accurate estimation of the state-of-charge (SOC) of the onboard lithium-ion battery is crucial in ensuring the safe, efficient, and reliable operation of the battery management system (BMS). This research suggests a novel approach for striking a reasonable balance between the POCUS battery management systems computation time and SOC estimate accuracy. Given that the lithium-ion battery is a non-linear and time-varying system, it is modelled by a second-order RC equivalent circuit model (2-RC ECM). Real-time parametric identification is carried out using the method of recursive least squares with respect to an adaptive forgetting factor (AFFRLS). The joint SOC evaluation technique, ordering the results of real-time identification and the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) recommended for estimating battery SOC at temperatures between 0 and 45 °C. Simulations were carried out using experimental data from the DST (Dynamic Stress Test), incremental current and, the Federal Urban Driving Program (FUDS) of the CALCE research group to evaluate the proposed approach. Based on the monitoring of dynamic changes in model parameters and the computational complexity of existing algorithms, the results reveal that the joint AFFRLS-EKF estimation proposed for the SOC strategy achieves good accuracy, high adaptability and rapid convergence of SOC estimation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Battery model
Online identification
Join state-of-charge estimation
Point-of-care ultrasound system (POCUS)
Battery management system

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title:
Identification of the
S O C − O C V − T
curve
Joint estimation of state of charge (SOC) with extended Kalman filter (EKF)
Analysis of real-time state-of-charge estimation parameters

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow chart of the AFFRLS-EKF method.
(a) Approximation of the true OCV-SOC curve at 25 ℃. (b) OCV-SOC curves at different temperatures. (c) OCV error at 0 and 45 ℃. (d) OCV-SOC-T curves.

Comparison between the estimated voltage and the reference voltage at 25 °C for the (a) FUDS and (b) DST profiles. Battery terminal voltage identification errors at 25 °C for (c) FUDS and (d) DST profiles. (e) MAE and (f) RMSE of battery terminal voltage at 25 °C for FUDS and DST profiles.
2-RC ECM dynamic identification results in real time of (a) resistors and (b) capacitors.
Results of the SOC estimation for the (a) FUDS and (b) DST profiles. (c) MAE and (d) RMSE of the battery SOC estimation at 25 °C for the FUDS and DST profiles.

head.ce:title: Lithium-ion point-of-care ultrasound battery joint state of charge estimation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Higher Technical Teachers’ Training College of the University of Ebolowa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With recent advances in ultrasound technology, including accessibility and portability, point-of-care ultrasound systems (POCUS) are becoming ubiquitous in urban and rural health services. The efficient use of POCUS battery power is necessary due to the limited access to electricity in rural locations. Accurate estimation of the state-of-charge (SOC) of the onboard lithium-ion battery is crucial in ensuring the safe, efficient, and reliable operation of the battery management system (BMS). This research suggests a novel approach for striking a reasonable balance between the POCUS battery management systems computation time and SOC estimate accuracy. Given that the lithium-ion battery is a non-linear and time-varying system, it is modelled by a second-order RC equivalent circuit model (2-RC ECM). Real-time parametric identification is carried out using the method of recursive least squares with respect to an adaptive forgetting factor (AFFRLS). The joint SOC evaluation technique, ordering the results of real-time identification and the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) recommended for estimating battery SOC at temperatures between 0 and 45 °C. Simulations were carried out using experimental data from the DST (Dynamic Stress Test), incremental current and, the Federal Urban Driving Program (FUDS) of the CALCE research group to evaluate the proposed approach. Based on the monitoring of dynamic changes in model parameters and the computational complexity of existing algorithms, the results reveal that the joint AFFRLS-EKF estimation proposed for the SOC strategy achieves good accuracy, high adaptability and rapid convergence of SOC estimation.

ce:section.ce:para: With recent developments in ultrasound technology, Point-Of-Care Ultrasound Scanners (POCUS) are rapidly becoming ubiquitous in various specialities in medicine [1,2]. Their great potential in emergency, intensive care and especially rural areas have generated great interest among many clinicians in both developed and developing countries [3,4]. The main advantages of these ultrasound scanners are their small size for easy transport, lower cost and easy integration with smartphones, tablets and the cloud [5]. In these ultrasound scanners, lithium-ion batteries are more convenient because they have a long life, high energy density (150–280 W.h/kg), high power density (200–300 W/kg), low cost, low self-discharge coefficient and no memory effect [6]. However, there are still concerns about their autonomy, reliability, durability, and safety in rural areas. The lithium-ion battery behaves like a non-linear structure, fluctuating over time during operation [7,8]. Therefore, offline identification methods are not suitable. In addition, during fast charging, low level of electric power quality in rural areas and high temperatures can cause irreversible chemical reactions in the battery [9,10]. Monitoring battery behaviour through functions such as SOC estimation is predominant for increasing the efficiency and safety performance of the battery. There are several contributions to improving the prowess of SOC estimation depending on battery model, identifying the parameters of the chosen battery model method, and the SOC estimation method. The most prominent are analysed in the following.

The SOC of a battery is determined by the relationship between its current remaining capacity and its available capacity [11]. It is usually estimated during battery operation as a sensor cannot measure it. The accuracy evaluation of the SOC estimation of the battery will involve the choice of its equivalent model, the identification in real time of the parameters of the chosen model, the estimation of the SOC according to two test profiles, and finally the calculation and evaluation of the errors.

The battery model is a crucial entity that acts on the accuracy of the estimation. Frequently used models are the ECM [12], the electrochemical model [7] and data-driven black box models [13]. However, in practice, many systems use models based on the ECM because they are simpler and more practical for systems with low computing power [14]. The main equivalent circuit representation models are the
R
i n t
model [15], the New Generation Vehicle Partnership (NGVP) model [16], the Thevenin model [17], and the second-order RC equivalent circuit model (2-RC ECM) [18]. The
R
i n t
model is a simple model consisting of a DC voltage source in series with internal resistance. However, it does not represent the state of the internal reactions in the battery. The PNGV model is characterised by a more accurate representation of the battery discharge process. The dynamic RC ECM of order
n th
traces the relationship among the internal elements of the battery and the temperature. Nevertheless, the complexity increases with the model’s order, and the battery’s evolution becomes difficult to assess in real time. A comparative study on the accuracy, complexity, and robustness of 12 ECM was carried out by [19]. It has been found that the two-resistor–capacitor (RC) network model is more suitable for LiFePo4 and LiMnCo batteries. Therefore, the 2 RC ECM model is commonly used, not only for its good accuracy and dynamic properties in simulation, but also for its low complexity [20].

However, with the complexity of chemical reactions and the internal physical structure of lithium-ion batteries, the internal battery level depends on ambient temperature, current, capacity and age. The model’s accuracy no longer depends only on its structure but also on the algorithm’s accuracy in identifying its parameters. It is then necessary to determine the parameters corresponding to the model that is closest to reality, with a good degree of accuracy. Several parametric identification methods are used, including recursive least square (RLS) [21,22], Kalman filters (KF) [23], genetic algorithms [24] and particle swarm optimisation [25]. The RLS method is one of the most widely used. It searches for the best match between the data functions and determines the unknown solution using the minimisation of the sum of the mean square errors defined by the estimated and measured values. For this method, the increase in data during the recursive process of battery operation and the frequent variation of current during charging and discharging lead to large errors. [26,27] propose methods based on RLS with a fixed forgetting factor (FFRLS) and adaptive forgetting factor (AFFRLS) that allow the identification to be adapted to the evolution of the battery operation to solve these problems. Unlike FFRLS, AFFRLS uses very little memory and gives a robust performance in parameter identification established on the equations of the state of the internal dynamics of the battery model.

In the literature, there are several approaches to estimating the SOC, including an open circuit voltage (OCV) method, Coulomb count method and ampere-hour count method [28], the model-based methods [29] and intelligent methods based on artificial neural networks [28] and fuzzy logic [30]. Each of these methods has its advantages and limitations [31]. The methods that consider the battery model are the most popular because they de facto eliminate errors, reduce computational power and increase real-time performance but require an accurate model. Once the battery model is established, the most common methods for estimating the SOC include Kalman filters [21,22,32], particle filters [33], H-infinity filters [8], and sliding mode observers [34] algorithms. The goal of the KF family of algorithms, which are the most popular online adaptive estimation algorithms, is to minimise the variance while maximising the state estimate of dynamic systems [35,36]. Only time-dependent linear models are suitable for the KF method. The widely used extended Kalman filter (EKF), unscented Kalman filter (UKF) and sigma-point Kalman filter (SPKF) algorithms are examples of advanced variants of the KF algorithm that researchers are developing to extend its use in nonlinear battery systems [21,37]. These algorithms offer a number of advantages, such as improved performance in non-linear systems or with non-Gaussian noise distributions, including self-correction, dynamic estimation of the SOC error range, and adaptive reduction of the influence of measurement and sensor noise [21]. Therefore, the EKF-based estimation algorithm is used in this paper to estimate the battery SOC.

In this paper, to achieve a good trade-off between real-time capacity and accurate estimation, we propose a real-time SOC estimation method centred on the junction between the AFFRLS approach for model parameter identification and the EKF algorithm for SOC estimation. First, we use the second-order RC equivalence circuit model for an accurate representation of the internal dynamics of the battery and obtain the corresponding equations of state. Unlike FFRLS, AFFRLS uses very little memory and gives robust performance in identifying parameters on the basis of the equations of state of the internal dynamics of the battery model. Therefore, we propose the AFFRLS-EKF SOC joint estimation procedure for a lithium-ion battery. AFFRLS and EKF will be employed to identify the battery model parameters and estimate the SOC in real time at each iteration for different operating temperatures (
T ∈
0
45
℃), respectively. The AFFRLS-EKF approach can be applied to the joint estimation of the SOC under the practical operating conditions of a POCUS. This method is then verified using FUDS and DST tests obtained from the Centre for Advanced Life Cycle Engineering (CALCE) experimental database. The results reveal that the AFFRLS-EKF approach to joint SOC estimation achieves good accuracy, good adaptability and rapid convergence of the SOC under different operating conditions.

The remainder of this work is structured as follows. Section “Battery modelling” describes the battery model, its equation of state and the real-time method for parameter identification; section “Joint estimation of state of charge (SOC) with Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)” presents the proposed method for joint SOC estimation; section “Results and discussion” presents the opted experimental database and the results obtained, and section “Conclusion” concludes the paper.

This section first illustrates the dynamic modelling and the battery state model. The real time approach to identifying the model parameters, including
U
o c v
= f
( S O C , T )
and the AFFRLS algorithm, are then presented.

Equivalent battery circuit models describing static and dynamic behaviour with excellent accuracy are made up of the basic electronic components. The 2-RC ECM is used to balance real-time performance and estimation accuracy. This is illustrated in Fig. 1.

It has an ideal voltage source
U
O C V
(open circuit voltage), a resistor
R
0
(internal resistance) and two parallel RC cells.
U
b
is the voltage at the battery terminals. The R-C cells describe the electrochemical reactions due to polarisation and the effects of its concentration on the battery.

The credibility of the battery model depends on the accuracy of the parameters. The parameters to be identified are
U
O C V
, the equivalent internal resistance
R
0
, the polarisation resistances
R
1
and

C

. In this study, the relationship between
U
O C V
and temperature is established by a polynomial fitting curve, and the other parameters are identified by the AFFRLS method.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Identification of the
S O C − O C V − T
curve
ce:section.ce:para:
An electrochemical functional relationship is established with
S O C
, assuming that
U
O C V
is equal to the electromotive force and characterises the energy of the battery.
(15)
U
O C V
= f
( S O C )
.

The incremental current test (ICT) allows adjusting the relationship parameters between
U
O C V
and
S O C
. This relationship is a polynomial fitting function of order 9 and is defined by :
(16)
U
O C V
=
a
1
S O
C
9

9

a
10
.

The
O C V − S O C
curve of the battery changes as the external temperature changes and the battery ages.
It is therefore necessary to update the coefficients of (16) and to incorporate the changes in the parameters in the equivalent circuit.

The coefficients of (16) are then defined as a quadratic function of temperature by
(17)
a
1
a
2
…
a
10
T
= Λ x
T
2
T
1
T
,
where
a
i
are the coefficients of the electrochemical relationship, and A is the constant matrix obtained from ICT experimental data.

The results and discussion section shows the
U
O C V
− S O C
curve with the effects of temperature.

There are two methods for determining the parameters for the chosen battery model: Offline identification, achieved through charge and discharge experiments. Unfortunately, this method only supplies set parameters unable to be adjusted by the influence of changes in temperature and age. As a result, continuous use will inevitably lead to an imprecise model. During online identification, the battery’s current state determines the pre-set parameters. Updating is then accomplished based on the substantial operating conditions, enabling accurate observation and real-time estimation of the
S O C
.

The RLS method is a popular technique for determining system parameters. It focuses on using the discrete square-normed function to the metric image. However, FFRLS, which succeeds RLS, incorporates a forgetting factor λ into the matrix of observed data and its output vector.

The FFRLS algorithm, based on recursive least squares, is used in this paper for online parameter identification. Let
θ
k
=
θ
1
θ
2
θ
3
θ
4
θ
5
T
and
φ
k
=
[
E
k − i
I
k − i
]
T
(
i = 1 , 2
),
ϕ
( k )
representing the known data at time k and
θ
( k )
denoting the parameter requiring estimation at time k .

The given initial value is typically a small, real matrix. Prior to the start of the algorithm, both input current and
U
O C V
are zero, with
U
O C V
being equal to the battery terminal voltage. Therefore,
E
( 1 )
= E
( 2 )
= 0
, and the initial value of
φ
( 0 )
=
0
0
0
0
0
. The gain matrix
K
0
is calculated using the following equation:
(18)
K
0

P

is the covariance matrix at time k , with an initial value of an identity matrix. Consequently, the estimator

(19)

L

−

,
The open circuit voltage at time k can be determined by the polynomial between
U

and
S O C
.

is substituted into (18) to obtain the gain matrix

. The substitution of
K
0
into (19) gives

. The estimated values of
R

,

at time k can be obtained through calculations using Eqs. (10) and (11).

The gain matrix,
K

, is used to update the covariance matrix

( k − 1 )

In the next moment, the parameters are identified again.
I
d
are identity matrices. The flowchart in Fig. 2 outlines the successive steps of implementing the AFFRLS algorithm in real-time for updating model parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Joint estimation of state of charge (SOC) with extended Kalman filter (EKF)
ce:section.ce:para: After identifying battery parameters, state variables are estimated and updated by measuring terminal voltages, temperatures and currents. 1In order to synchronise the updating of the model parameters with the state of charge (SOC), this paper uses the AFFRLS-EKF joint estimation method. The Kalman filter, a recursive estimation algorithm incorporating filter theory and least squares error, is used. It compares estimates of current state and previous state, updates state variables, and calculates predictions for current state. Although the traditional Kalman filter algorithm is appropriate for linear systems, it is not applicable to non-linear systems such as batteries and therefore requires modification.

Based on the Kalman filter algorithm, the extended Kalman filter (EKF) linearises the system under study by eliminating high-order nonlinear terms through Taylor expansion. The algorithm can be expressed as follows for any nonlinear system:
(21)
x
k + 1
= f
(
x
k
,
u
k
)
+
w
k
,
y
k
= g
(
x
k
,
u
k
)
+
v
k
,

Here,
f
(
x
k
,
u
k
)
represents the evolution function of the state and
g
(
x
k
,
u
k
)
the observation function, where our specific case is the battery terminal voltage. The state vector at time k is denoted by
x
k
and the observation value of the system is represented by
y
k
. The input vector of the system at time k is denoted by
u
k
, while
w
k
and
v
k
represent independent white noise. By conducting the Taylor expansion of the previous equation, we can establish the general form of the state model as presented in the following equation.
(22)
x
k + 1
=
A
k
x
k
+
B
k
u
k
+
w
k
,
y
k
=
C
k
x
k
+
D
k
u
k
+
v
k
,
where
x
k
,
u
k
,
y
k
,
A
k
,
B
k
,
C
k
and
D
k
represent the state variable, input variable, output variable, state matrix, input matrix, observation matrix and input–output matrix, respectively. Process noise generated by modelling error is represented by
w
k
, and measurement noise generated by current and voltage measurement chain by
v
k
.

The battery model (14) identifies the following Kalman parameters
(23)
x
k
=
S O C
( k )
U
1
( k )
U
2
( k )
T
(24)
u
k
= I
( k )
(25)
y
k
=
U
b
( k )
(26)

=
1
0
0

exp
−
Δ T

Δ T

=
−
η Δ T
3600 . Q
R

Δ T

Δ T

k
=
d

O C V
d S O C
− 1
− 1
(29)

k
= −
R
0
The flow diagram of the proposed methodology to estimate battery SOC using experimental data and an extended Kalman filter is shown in Fig. 3.

To validate the efficacy of the proposed method for joint estimation of
S O C
, simulations have been conducted using experimental data. A presentation of the database is given first in this section. Later, the incremental current test (ICT) is used to identify the coefficients of
U
o c v
. Subsequently, we evaluate the efficacy of the proposed approach by scrutinising the
S O C
errors and the voltage of the battery terminals.

This paper uses the experimental data published by the CALCE (Center for Advanced Life Cycle Engineering) research group at the University of Maryland in the USA [38]. The experimental setup, represented in Fig. 4(a), comprises an Arbin BT2000 Battery Test System that oversees and documents the battery’s charging and discharging cycles, in addition to a thermal chamber that captures the surrounding temperature of the battery.

The database is based on a Samsung INR 18650-20R battery cell, the factory specifications of which are shown in the Table 1. The battery is of the LiNiMnCo type and has a maximum capacity of
2000
mAh
. The database includes test data at three distinct temperatures, specifically 0 ℃, 25 ℃ and 45 ℃ [38].

DST and FUDS, as described in the USABC Electric Vehicle Battery Test Manual, are two standard battery operating conditions. The dataset contains DST and FUDS test data consisting of two battery levels, one at 50% and the other at 80% of total remaining capacity. These determine the initial state of the battery undergoing the DST or FUDS test.
Figs. 4(c) and 4(d) show the current and voltage profiles taken from the DST and FUDS processes at 25 ℃.

The test consists of several SOC and resting intervals. After discharging or recharging at specified SOC intervals (e.g. intervals of 10% SOC), the battery is allowed to rest to approach a near-steady-state equilibrium. Fig. 4(b) displays the current and voltage profiles during the test.

The recorded data allow us to construct the OCV-SOC-T relationship. This non-linear relationship is fitted by interpolation using 9th-order polynomial functions for better accuracy at different temperatures in the MATLAB software. The relation (30) gives us the different coefficients of the polynomial fitting functions as a function of temperature(
T ∈
0
45
℃).

The shift between the
S O C − O C V
curves at different temperatures (see Fig. 5) shows that their coefficients depend on the temperature. In addition to battery temperature, battery model identification is based on voltage and current. Polynomial interpolation of order 2 is performed for each order of the fitting coefficient of the
S O C − O C V
curves. The surface curve in Fig. 5(d) represents the evolution of the
S O C − O C V − T
relationship between 0 and
45
℃.
(30)
a
1
= − 0 . 3394
T
2
+ 30 . 935 T − 506 . 825
a
2
= 1 . 88865
T
2
− 136 . 19 T + 1401 . 35
a
3
= − 4 . 5261
T
2
+ 252 . 794 . T − 521 . 01
a
4
= 6 . 08283
T
2
− 258 . 4509 T − 2338 . 5
a
5
= − 5 . 0142
T
2
+ 160 . 2142 T + 3821
a
6
= 2 . 6145
T

− 63 . 3012 T − 2677
a
7
= − 0 . 8597
T
2
+ 16 . 7217 T + 1008 . 9
a

= 0 . 1712
T

− 3 . 0714 T − 208 . 4

= − 0 . 0187
T

+ 0 . 3742 T + 22 . 07
a
10
= 0 . 0008532
T
2
− 0 . 02205 T + 2 . 6265

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of real-time state-of-charge estimation parameters
ce:section.ce:para:
When identifying model parameters, accuracy increases as the estimated output voltage aligns with the measured battery terminal voltage. Evaluating accuracy and tracking capability can be done by assessing the error between the model’s output voltage and the measured battery terminal voltage. This also serves as a reflection of the accuracy of the model. The assessment measures employed in this research comprise the mean absolute error (MAE) and the root mean square error (RMSE). Specifically, the RMSE of the foregoing error is employed to evaluate the accuracy of the model as well as the superiority of the algorithm. The formula for calculation is as follows:
(31)
M A E =
∑
i = 1
N
|
y
k
−
y
k
ˆ
|
N
,
(32)
R M S E =
1
N
∑
i = 1
N
|
y
k
−
y
k
ˆ
|
2
,
where
y
k
is the reference value provided by the database at the time k , and
y
ˆ
k
is the output value calculated by our method at the time k .

The accuracy and parameters of the model are evaluated utilising various test profiles (DST and FUDS) at a temperature of 25 ℃. Figs. 6(a) and 6(b) illustrate the error of the battery terminal voltage, as determined by the AFFRLS method. For each profile, the mean absolute error (MAE) increases with increasing battery discharge depth, due to battery non-linearity and significant variation in
U
O C V
. The highest values observed were
25
mV
and
42 . 7
mV
for the FUDS and DST profiles respectively (see Figs. 6(c) and 6(d)).

In addition, the results show that the established temperature-dependent second-order RC model offers significant advantages in accurately predicting system behaviour over a wide temperature range. This model not only captures the dynamic response of thermal systems with high accuracy, but also takes into account the effects of temperature variations on system performance. As a result of the incorporation of second-order dynamics, this model not only provides accurate output voltage, but also has good robustness to temperature variations.

Figs. 6(e) and 6(f) display the mean absolute error (MAE) and root-mean-square error (RMSE) resulting from variable parameter identification at a temperature of 25 ℃for the numerous profiles under consideration. The outcome proves that the model, when combined with the proposed joint estimation method, exhibits the capability to track distinct dynamics across the entire state of charge (SOC) range with minimal error. This discrepancy is more pronounced between
10 – 20 %
and
40 – 50 %
SOC. The absence of hysteresis voltage consideration in the battery function below 30% SOC is a justifiable explanation for this [21].

Fig. 7 shows the real-time identification results of the resistances and capacitances of the ECM 2-RC using our joint estimation method. These results show that the equivalent internal resistance
R
0
, the slow dynamic resistance
R
1
, and the fast dynamic resistance
R
2
each vary very little, around a constant value, over the entire range of the identification process. Despite the sudden changes in the current profile values, the resistance and capacitance values vary slightly due to the automatic, real-time update of the adaptive forgetting factor based on the error between the OCV and the estimated real-time battery terminal voltage. The automatic, real-time update of the adaptive forgetting factor also significantly reduces the memory reserved for saving battery-operating data at previous times of the FFRLS method.

Figs. 8(a) and 8(b) show that the proposed joint estimation method tracks the reference charge state with reasonable accuracy, in agreement with the theoretical true value whose is calculated using the Coulomb counting method based on laboratory data [39]. The comparisons between the proposed AFFRLS-EKF charge state estimation results and the reference charge state of the experimental data for the FUDS and DST profiles at 50% load are presented in Figs. 8(c) and 8(d). The RMSE and MAE of the SOC over the whole range of the state of discharge have the maximum values at
25
℃ of 1.18% and 1.12%, and 1.11% and 1.09%, respectively, for FUDS and DST. The results are better than the estimation method used in [35,37].

The results show that the proposed joint estimation of SOC method can follow the dynamics of the FUDS and DST profiles with good scalability and fast convergence while guaranteeing an excellent state-of-charge estimation thanks to the real-time identification of the battery’s ECM 2-RC parameters.

This paper proposes and implements a joint AFFRLS-EKF method for estimating the SOC of a lithium-ion battery in this study. The AFFRLS method is used recursively for real-time identification of the battery model parameters. An adaptive forgetting factor is updated at each iteration based on the error between the OCV and the estimated battery terminal voltage. This identification method is combined with the EKF algorithm for real time estimating of the battery SOC. The robustness and efficiency of the proposed method were verified using experimental test data of a LiNiMnCo lithium-ion battery from the CALCE research group following the FUDS and DST profiles. By incorporating second-order dynamics, this model not only provides accurate output voltage, but also has good robustness to temperature variations. The results obtained show that the proposed joint estimation of SOC method can follow the various dynamics of the FUDS and DST profiles with good scalability and fast convergence while guaranteeing a good SOC estimation based on the real time identification of the 2-RC ECM parameters of the battery. The model combined with our proposed joint estimation method follows different dynamics, but with a more significant error between 10 and 20% of the SOC. Future work will consider the voltage hysteresis in the battery model and assess its impact on computation time and estimation accuracy.

Nicolas Daniel Mbele Ndzana: Design, Elaboration of ideas, Methodology, Writing and development of the application, Implementation of the algorithms and Matlab/Simulink. Aristide Tolok Nelem: Proofreading, Validation, Supervision, Verification of hypotheses and implementation of the algorithms and Matlab/Simulink. Yannick Antoine Abanda: Proofreading, Simulation validation in the digital environment. Mathieu Jean Pierre Pesdjock: Editing, Validation of simulation, theory, verification objectives and acceptance of results, Proofreading and approval of results. Murele Vanina Toukam Ngouagna: Verification, Validation, Proofreading and approval are necessary steps for ensuring the accuracy and reliability of the results. Odile Fernande Zeh: Coordination, Proofreading, Validation the algorithm, Adoption of the results and supervision. Pierre Ele: Proofreading, Validating algorithms and supervising environmental design in Matlab/Simulink.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The multi-innovation extended Kalman filter algorithm for battery SOC estimation
Ionics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Li, Y. Yang, D. Wang, S. Yin, The multi-innovation extended Kalman filter algorithm for battery SOC estimation, Ionics 26 (12) (2020) 6145–6156. doi:10.1007/s11581-020-03716-0.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Particle filter-based state-of-charge estimation and remaining-dischargeable-time prediction method for lithium-ion batteries
J. Power Sources

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Chen, H. Sun, G. Dong, J. Wei, J. Wu, Particle filter-based state-of-charge estimation and remaining-dischargeable-time prediction method for lithium-ion batteries, Journal of Power Sources 414 (2019) 158–166. doi:10.1016/j.jpowsour.2019.01.012.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel approach for state of charge estimation based on adaptive switching gain sliding mode observer in electric vehicles
J. Power Sources

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Chen, W. Shen, Z. Cao, A. Kapoor, A novel approach for state of charge estimation based on adaptive switching gain sliding mode observer in electric vehicles, Journal of Power Sources 246 (2014) 667–678. doi:10.1016/j.jpowsour.2013.08.039.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Research on co-estimation algorithm of SOC and SOH for lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles
Electronics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.-Q. Du, J.-B. Shao, D.-M. Wu, Z. Ren, Z.-Y. Wu, W.-Q. Ren, Research on Co-Estimation Algorithm of SOC and SOH for Lithium-Ion Batteries in Electric Vehicles, Electronics 11 (2) (2022) 181. doi:10.3390/electronics11020181.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium-Ion battery parameters and state of charge joint estimation using bias compensation least squares and the alternate algorithm
Math. Probl. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Xiong, Y. Mo, C. Yan, Lithium-Ion Battery Parameters and State of Charge Joint Estimation Using Bias Compensation Least Squares and the Alternate Algorithm, Mathematical Problems in Engineering 2020 (2020) e1757384. doi:10.1155/2020/1757384.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Joint estimation of SOC and available capacity of power lithium-ion battery
Electronics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Huang, C. Liu, M. Hu, L. Li, G. Jin, H. Yang, Joint Estimation of SOC and Available Capacity of Power Lithium-Ion Battery, Electronics 11 (1) (2022) 151. doi:10.3390/electronics11010151.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Error analysis of the model-based state-of-charge observer for lithium-ion batteries
IEEE Trans. Veh. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Shen, M. Ouyang, X. Han, X. Feng, L. Lu, J. Li, Error analysis of the model-based state-of-charge observer for lithium-ion batteries, IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology 67 (9) (2018) 8055–8064. doi:10.1109/TVT.2018.2842820.
